era: 1st Century AD, Capernaum tax booth / Galilee

visual_dna.age_range: ~30-40 — established enough in his career to have a house large enough for a feast
visual_dna.ethnicity: 1st Century Jewish — but employed by the Roman tax system, making him a collaborator
visual_dna.skin_tone: olive, indoor life — a tax collector sits at a booth, not in the fields
visual_dna.hair_texture: medium, groomed — he can afford to care about appearance
visual_dna.beard_style: neat, trimmed beard — prosperity visible in grooming
visual_dna.eye_color: dark, sharp — used to calculating and reading people
visual_dna.height_build: medium build, not laborer's physique — an indoor occupation
distinguishing_marks: sitting at a tax booth on the Capernaum road when Jesus walks by
wealthy enough to host a great feast immediately after being called
the feast is full of 'tax collectors and sinners' — his social world
he was despised by his own people — Jewish tax collectors were seen as traitors

wardrobe_by_arc.tax_booth: better quality robes than most — possibly Roman-influenced dress, practical but prosperous
wardrobe_by_arc.feast_host: finer robes at his own feast — a man in his home showing his wealth
wardrobe_by_arc.after_calling: traveling disciple clothes — he left the booth and the income

voice_profile.register: educated baritone — tax collecting required literacy and numeracy, unusual for the era
voice_profile.pace: measured, precise — a man who counts carefully
voice_profile.accent_note: possibly slightly more cosmopolitan than the Galilean fishermen — his work connected him to Romans
speech_mannerisms: Jesus calls him and he 'got up, left everything and followed him' — Luke says no more. His obedience is wordless.
the feast he throws is immediate and lavish — he responds with his whole life
Luke does not record Matthew speaking — he is a watcher, a recorder, even in the narrative
voice_profile.notes: In Luke he is called Levi. He later appears as Matthew in the apostle list. The same man — but Luke preserves his birth name, not the name Jesus may have given him.

emotional_arc.opening: Prosperous, isolated. Wealthy but socially dead among his own people. Sitting at the booth is his whole world.
emotional_arc.midpoint: Jesus walks by and says two words: 'Follow me.' He leaves everything — the booth, the income, the structure of his life.
emotional_arc.climax: The feast — he throws open his house to everyone like him. The Pharisees are appalled. Jesus is at the table.
emotional_arc.resolution: He follows. He eventually writes down what he saw. The careful recorder finds his true calling.

evangelical_baptist.notes: The sinner called and transformed — his response is immediate and total
evangelical_baptist.marketing_angle: Two words. 'Follow me.' He got up and left everything. No negotiation.

catholic.notes: St. Matthew the Evangelist — his calling is an important feast day
catholic.marketing_angle: The tax collector who became an evangelist. The feast he threw is where it all turned.

black_church.notes: The collaborator who was redeemed — working within a corrupt system, called out of it
black_church.marketing_angle: He worked for the oppressor's system. Jesus walked up to his booth and called him out of it.

hispanic_christian.notes: The sinner welcomed at the table — the feast of fellowship
hispanic_christian.marketing_angle: His first act after being called was to throw a party. That's faith.

secular_curious.notes: The most complex calling — a collaborator with empire who abandons it immediately
secular_curious.marketing_angle: He worked for Rome, extracting money from his own people. Jesus walked past his booth and said his name. And he left.

youth_gen_z.notes: The person society wrote off as corrupt who turned out to be worth knowing
youth_gen_z.marketing_angle: Everyone had him written off. Everyone was wrong.

jewish_american.notes: The Jewish tax collector — the most hated category in 1st century Jewish society, and why
jewish_american.marketing_angle: Tax collectors were Jews working for Rome — seen as traitors. Jesus kept eating with them.

women.marketing_angle: He opened his house to everyone society rejected. The table was open.

men.notes: The man who left a lucrative career with two words — the radical call and the radical response
men.marketing_angle: He had a good income, a structured life, a place in the system. He left it all in five seconds.

audience_targeting.modern_parallel: The successful person working in a corrupt industry who leaves everything when something real walks in
audience_targeting.modern_parallel_detail: He was not stupid or unaware of the moral compromises his job required. He was complicit in the system. Then Jesus walked by.

ai_generation.consistency_seed_prompt: 1st century Jewish tax collector man age 30-40, olive skin, neat dark hair, trimmed beard, medium build, better quality robes than fishermen, sitting at a stone toll booth on a Capernaum road, sharp calculating eyes, cinematic photoreal, bright midday Galilean light, 8k
ai_generation.negative_prompt: fisherman's rough clothes, extremely wealthy Roman dress, cartoonish, European features, modern clothing
ai_generation.midjourney_style_params: cinematic photoreal, bright Galilean daylight, toll booth setting, period-accurate prosperity
ai_generation.firefly_prompt: Photorealistic portrait of a 1st century Jewish tax collector, age 30-40, neat dark hair and trimmed beard, olive skin, wearing better quality robes than the average worker, sitting at a stone toll booth on a Capernaum road, sharp eyes, expression of calculation changing to surprise, bright midday light
reference_films: The Chosen TV series — Levi/Matthew arc across season 1
ai_generation.color_grading_notes: Tax booth scene: bright, commercial, midday — the ordinary world before the call. Feast scene: warm interior lamps, celebratory.

production_notes: The calling of Levi is one of the shortest scenes in Luke (three verses) and one of the most powerful. The feast that immediately follows must feel lavish — he held nothing back.
